1: Devar-YHWH asher hayah el-Hoshea ben-Beeri -- 'The word of YHWH that came to Hosea son of Beeri.' The superscription anchors the book in both prophetic authority (devar-YHWH, the word of YHWH) and historical precision: the reigns listed span ca. 793-715 BCE in the south (Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, Hezekiah) and ca. 793-753 BCE in the north (Jeroboam II). Hosea's ministry in the northern kingdom is the sole book of the Latter Prophets whose primary audience is Israel (not Judah). Hoshea (H1954) means 'salvation' -- the same root as Joshua and Jesus (Yeshua). The name of the prophet whose message is overwhelmingly one of judgment carries the embedded promise that judgment is not YHWH's final word.
2: Techillat dibber-YHWH be-Hoshea -- 'When the LORD first spoke through Hosea.' The opening of Hosea's prophetic career is immediately scandalous. Lekh qach-lekha eshet zenutim -- 'Go, take for yourself a wife of unfaithfulness.' Zenutim (H2183, fornication/prostitution/unfaithfulness) is the key term: whether this refers to a woman who was already a prostitute or to one who would become unfaithful is debated in the tradition. The MKT reads 'a wife of unfaithfulness' as describing her character rather than her profession. Ki-zanoh tizhneh ha-aretz me-acharei YHWH -- 'for the land commits great fornication by departing from YHWH.' The marriage is enacted prophecy: Hosea's domestic experience maps onto YHWH's covenant grief. The verb zanah (to play the whore) is applied to Israel's relationship with YHWH -- the covenant as marriage, apostasy as adultery.
3: Va-yelekh va-yiqach et-Gomer bat-Divlaim -- 'So he went and married Gomer daughter of Diblaim.' Hosea's obedience is immediate and unqualified. Gomer (H1586) -- a name from the Table of Nations (Genesis 10:2-3) suggesting a northern or foreign association. Divlaim (H1691) -- 'double fig-cake'; the name has been taken as symbolic (sweetness leading to unfaithfulness) by some commentators, but it is more likely a personal name. Va-tahar va-teled lo ben -- 'and she conceived and bore him a son.' The birth narrative is terse and businesslike -- the focus is on the name and its meaning rather than the child himself. The pattern of the three children (son-daughter-son) with their symbolic names structures the rest of Hosea 1.
4: Vayomer YHWH elav qera shemo Yizreel -- 'And the LORD said to him, Name him Jezreel.' Yizreel (H3157, 'God sows') is simultaneously a place name (the great valley where Jehu massacred the house of Ahab, 2 Kgs 9-10) and a word containing the promise of restoration ('God sows' = God will scatter and then re-sow his people). Ki-od meat ufaqadti et-demei Yizreel al-beit Yehu -- 'for in a little while I will punish the house of Jehu for the blood of Jezreel.' The very act that was approved in 2 Kings (Jehu's coup) is now declared a blood-guilt: either YHWH's assessment changed with the generations, or the approved act was nevertheless violent beyond what was required. Ve-hishbatti mamlekhet beit-Yisrael -- 'and I will bring the kingdom of the house of Israel to an end.' The northern dynasty's end (722 BCE) is prophesied.
5: Ve-hayah ba-yom ha-hu -- 'On that day.' The prophetic formula of eschatological reversal introduces the judgment. Ve-shabarti et-qeshet Yisrael be-emeq Yizreel -- 'I will break the bow of Israel in the valley of Jezreel.' The qeshet (bow) was Israel's primary military weapon; its breaking in the valley of Jezreel -- where Jehu's dynasty began -- announces both military defeat and dynastic termination. The bow's breaking appears again in 2:18 as a peace promise: the bow will be removed from the land when covenant is restored. What is here a judgment will there be a gift.
6: Va-tahar od va-teled bat -- 'She conceived again and bore a daughter.' The second child is female, marking a shift in the symbolic structure. Vayomer lo qeri shemah Lo-Ruhamah -- 'And God said to him, Name her Lo-ruhamah -- Not Pitied/Not Loved.' Ruhamah from racham (H7355, to have compassion/show mercy) -- the same root as rechem (womb) and rachamim (compassion/mercy). The name denies the foundational maternal compassion: YHWH will no longer show the compassion of a parent for a child. Ki lo osif od arachem et-beit Yisrael -- 'for I will no longer have mercy on the house of Israel.' The od (again/any more) marks the rupture: what was Israel's covenant possession (divine mercy) is being withdrawn. But the name's reversal in Hosea 2:23 ('I will have mercy on Lo-ruhamah') is the book's promise.
7: Ve-et-beit Yehudah arachem -- 'But I will have mercy on the house of Judah.' The contrast between Israel (north, Lo-ruhamah) and Judah (south, still under mercy) is a recurring structural feature. Ve-hoshati otam ba-YHWH Eloheihem -- 'and I will save them by the LORD their God.' The divine name is emphatic -- YHWH himself as Savior, not any military power. Ve-lo ohoshiam be-qeshet u-ve-cherev u-ve-milchamah be-susim u-ve-farashim -- 'I will not save them by bow or sword or warfare, by horses or horsemen.' The salvation of Judah (presumably the deliverance from Sennacherib in 701 BCE is in view) will be miraculous, not military -- echoing the Exodus pattern. The contrast foreshadows Hosea 2:18's covenant of peace.
8: Va-tigmol et-Lo-Ruhamah va-tahar va-teled ben -- 'After she weaned Lo-ruhamah, she conceived again and bore a son.' The verb gamal (to wean) marks the passage of time -- weaning in antiquity typically occurred around age two to three. Three children over approximately five to seven years. The third child receives the most devastating name. The pattern of Gomer's pregnancies mirrors Israel's progressive alienation from YHWH across the generations.
9: Va-yomer qera shemo Lo-Ammi -- 'And God said, Name him Lo-ammi -- Not My People.' Ammi (H5971, my people) -- the covenant-formula term. 'My people / your God' is the compact bilateral covenant declaration (cf. Leviticus 26:12; Jeremiah 7:23; Hosea 11:4; Hosea 30:22; Ezekiel 36:28). Its negation here is catastrophic: YHWH formally dissolves the covenant in its most essential formulation. Ki atem lo-ammi ve-anokhi lo-ehyeh lakhem -- 'for you are not my people, and I am not your God.' The phrase lo-ehyeh (I am not) is the negation of ehyeh (I AM) -- YHWH's own name from Exodus 3:14. The covenant God who identified himself as 'I AM' now declares 'I am not' to his people. This is the nadir of the covenant, making the reversal in 2:23 and its NT fulfillment (Romans 9:25-26; 1 Peter 2:10) all the more powerful.
10: Ve-hayah mispar benei-Yisrael ke-chol ha-yam -- 'Yet the number of the children of Israel will be like the sand of the sea.' Immediately after the covenant dissolution of Hosea 1:9, the promise breaks through -- a structural feature of Hosea throughout. Ke-chol ha-yam (like the sand of the sea) -- the Abrahamic promise of innumerable descendants (Genesis 22:17; Hosea 32:12). Despite Lo-ammi, the Abrahamic promise remains. Asher lo-yimmad ve-lo yissafer -- 'which cannot be measured or numbered.' Ve-hayah bi-mekom asher-yiamer lahem lo-ammi atem yiamer lahem benei El-Chai -- 'and in the place where it was said to them, You are not my people, there it shall be said to them, Sons of the living God.' The reversal: the place of rejection becomes the place of adoption. Paul quotes this verse in Romans 9:26 for the inclusion of Gentiles -- the 'not my people' becoming 'sons of the living God.' El Chai (the living God) contrasts with the dead idols Israel pursued.
11: Ve-niqvetzu benei Yehudah u-venei Yisrael yachdav -- 'The children of Judah and Israel will be gathered together.' The eschatological reunion of the divided kingdoms -- a promise that runs throughout the prophets (Isaiah 11:12-13; Jeremiah 31:1; Ezekiel 37:15-28). Ve-samu lahem rosh echad -- 'and they will appoint for themselves one head.' Rosh echad (one head) -- the messianic ruler who will unite the two kingdoms under one shepherd-king. Ve-alu min-ha-aretz -- 'and they will go up from the land.' The going-up suggests a new Exodus -- the dispersed gathered from the nations and returning to the land. Ki gadol yom Yizreel -- 'for great is the day of Jezreel.' The name Jezreel ('God sows') is now revalued: after the judgment of scattering, God re-sows his people in the land. The three children's names -- Lo-ammi, Lo-ruhamah, Jezreel -- are all reversed in the restoration promise.
